title: Sensitivity Enhancement near High-Order Exceptional Points via Dissipative Couplings
authors: Yuanjie Zhang, Jiaojiao Li, Zhihuang Luo
abstract: High-order exceptional points (EPs) emerging in non-Hermitian systems have attracted broad interest for their significantly enhanced sensitivity to perturbations. However, quantum sensing schemes based on high-order EPs remain scarce, due to the experimental challenge of fine-tuning the system to such an extremely sensitive isolated point. Here we propose a four-channel dissipative coupling model that supports both fourth-order exceptional surfaces and second-order exceptional volumes. This non-Hermitian model can be realized in a thermal atomic system, and its complex energy spectra can be determined via electromagnetically induced transparency spectroscopy. The proposed model exhibits a characteristic fourth-order response to multiple physical quantities such as the laser detuning and the distance between optical channels, significantly surpassing the response of second-order EPs. We further reveal the sensitivity-robustness trade-off under experimental noise. Our work opens a route toward high-performance sensing leveraging higher-order EPs.
summary: The paper proposes using a four-channel dissipative coupling model in an atomic system to achieve highly sensitive quantum sensing by exploiting high-order Exceptional Points (EPs). It demonstrates that fourth-order EPs offer superior sensitivity scaling compared to second-order EPs. Crucially, the work also analyzes the noise robustness trade-off, providing a comprehensive guide for realizing such sensors.
main_problem: The primary challenge is developing highly sensitive quantum sensing schemes by exploiting high-order Exceptional Points (EPs) in non-Hermitian systems while overcoming the experimental difficulty of tuning to isolated, extremely sensitive points.
main_result: The proposed four-channel dissipative coupling model supports fourth-order EPs over a broad parameter range, exhibiting significantly higher sensitivity enhancement compared to second-order EPs for small disturbances.
method: The analysis involves deriving an effective Hamiltonian from the system's dynamics (governed by master equations) and calculating the eigenvalue splitting ($\Delta\lambda$) near EP crossings using perturbation theory.
model_or_system: The model is a four-channel dissipative coupling system realized in a thermal atomic vapor cell using Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT). The dynamics are governed by an effective Hamiltonian derived from the full master equation for ground-state coherences.
key_observables: Sensitivity enhancement factor ($S$), eigenvalue splittings ($\Delta\lambda \propto |\epsilon|^{1/4}$ vs. $|\epsilon|^{1/2}$), and relative error ($E_r$) near EPs.
important_parameters:
Laser detunings ($\Delta_A, \Delta_B$), coupling strengths ($
u, \omega$), disturbance parameters ($\epsilon_{\Delta}, \epsilon_{v}$), and the order of the EP (second vs. fourth).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on adiabatic elimination of excited states in the EIT process; a later section notes that higher-order EPs are more susceptible to noise than second-order EPs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic model, the hypersurfaces defining different EP orders (Fig. 2), the scaling of eigenvalue splittings ($\propto |\epsilon|^{1/4}$ vs. $\propto |\epsilon|^{1/2}$) (Fig. 3), and comparisons of sensitivity enhancement ($S$) and relative error ($E_r$) for both second- and fourth-order EPs (Figs. 4 & 6).
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the general non-Hermitian model and its Hamiltonian structure, then details the physical realization using a four-channel dissipative coupling in an atomic system via EIT. The core analysis compares the sensitivity scaling and robustness of fourth-order versus second-order EPs under external disturbances.
why_interesting: This work directly applies advanced concepts from non-Hermitian physics (EPs) to a tangible AMO platform (cold atoms/EIT), providing quantitative metrics for sensing performance enhancement while also addressing the crucial issue of noise robustness, which is vital for experimental realization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a four-channel dissipative cou- pling model. Four optical channels with eigenfrequencies (∆A,∆B,−∆B,−∆A) are arranged in a chain and coupled dis- sipatively to their nearest neighbors with coupling strengths (iv, iw, iv).

FIG. 2. (a-c) Second-order EVs consisting of three hypersur- faces: Ξ1 = 0, Ξ2 = 0 and Π = 0 defined in the 4D parameter space {∆A, ∆B, v, w}, where v = uw. The colorbar represents the value of u. (d) Fourth-order ESs determined by the inter- section of two hypersurfaces Π = 0 and Φ = 0. The red points mark the locations of fourth-order EPs, corresponding to the fixed parameters used in (e) and (f). (e) and (f) Real and imaginary parts of complex eigenvalues λj (j = 1, 2, 3, 4) for fixed v = √

FIG. 3. Real and imaginary parts of the maximum and minimum eigenvalues λ1 and λ4 versus the eigenfrequency disturbance ϵ∆(a-d) and the coupling disturbance ϵv (e-h) near EPs. In (a), (b), (e), and (f), the system is tuned to a fourth-order EP with parameters ∆A = 3, ∆B = 1, v = √

FIG. 4. Sensitivity enhancement factor S as a function of the applied eigenfrequency disturbance ϵ∆(a) and the cou- pling disturbance ϵv (b). The red and blue solid curves repre- sent the sensitivity enhancement factors near the fourth- and second-order EPs, respectively.

FIG. 5. Scheme for implementing a fourth-order non- Hermitian model using a thermal 87Rb atomic-vapor cell. Four optical channels are arranged in a chain with alternating spacings (d1, d2, d1), each consisting of a spatially overlapped strong control beam and a weak probe beam. Within each channel, the atoms realize a standard Λ-type EIT involving two ground states |1⟩and |2⟩and an excited state |3⟩. The strong control fields with Rabi frequencies Ω(i) c for i = 1, 2, 3, 4 drive the transitions |1⟩→|3⟩, while the weak probe fields with Rabi frequencies Ω(i) p are on near resonance with the transitions |2⟩→|3⟩. The frequency differences between the control and probe fields are ∆A, ∆B, −∆B,...
